nativeRange.habitat: Small to medium-sized clear-water creeks and slow marginal zones with abundant aquatic vegetation; found in the lower Ucayali and Marañón river systems

decorPreferences: broad-leaved plants (Anubias, Java fern) for grazing and resting
driftwood and smooth rocks for aufwuchs colonisation
gentle flow — sponge or HOB filter with spray bar
soft substrate to prevent ventral abrasion

diet.notes: The Zebra Otocinclus is a specialized aufwuchs grazer — aufwuchs being the thin layer of algae, diatoms, bacteria, and microorganisms that coats submerged surfaces in mature aquariums. The species feeds almost exclusively on this biofilm rather than on free-floating or suspended food. Supplement natural grazing with sinking algae wafers, spirulina wafers, and blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, or spinach. Unlike common Otocinclus species, O. cocama rarely accepts protein-heavy foods. Because it feeds in short, frequent bursts throughout the day, a continuously available grazing surface is essential — specimens added to algae-stripped or newly established tanks tend to decline quickly.

goodWith: small peaceful characins (ember tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
avoidWith: large or aggressive fish that will outcompete for food or cause stress
fast-moving boisterous species that disturb biofilm colonies

imageCandidates.notes: Frank M. Greco; 640x417. Sharp side profile of a single live specimen clearly showing the diagnostic bold, wavy vertical dark bands running from dorsal midline to ventral flank on a light body; well-lit, in-colour, whole fish in frame.

Uncredited; 483x251. Group shot of two individuals (good for this shoaling species) with clear zebra-like vertical banding; correctly ID'd but lower resolution and slightly softer, so kept as non-recommended.

summary: The Zebra Otocinclus is a small Peruvian suckermouth catfish, reaching around 4.4 cm, distinguished by bold vertical dark bands — a stark contrast to the spotted pattern of common Otocinclus species. It requires a mature, biofilm-rich planted aquarium and is more sensitive and more expensive than typical Otocinclus.
careNotes: The Zebra Otocinclus (Otocinclus cocama) is endemic to the lower Ucayali and Marañón river drainages of Peru and is the most visually distinctive member of the genus, bearing bold vertically elongated blotches spanning from the dorsal midline to the ventral flank — a stark contrast to the spotted or striped patterns of O. vestitus and O. macrospilus. Adults reach 3.5–4.4 cm in length. Maintain soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12, temperature 21–25°C) and target high oxygen saturation with gentle filtration; a turnover of 4–5 times the tank volume per hour via a sponge filter or spray-bar hang-on-back filter is ideal. The species is an obligate aufwuchs grazer and must be introduced only into established aquariums with visible algae and biofilm colonising hard surfaces — avoid adding them to tanks under three months old, as newly cycled tanks lack the biofilm layer these fish depend on. Keep in groups of at least 6; solitary specimens tend to become stressed and waste away. Supplement grazing with sinking spirulina wafers, algae rounds, and blanched zucchini or cucumber placed on the substrate. Unlike common Otocinclus species, O. cocama seldom competes well for food alongside faster, more assertive bottom dwellers. The Zebra Oto commands a notably higher retail price (often US $10–20 or more per fish) than common Otocinclus species and is predominantly wild-caught; acclimate new arrivals extremely slowly (drip acclimation over 60 minutes or more) as they are sensitive to abrupt parameter shifts. The species is listed as Endangered by the IUCN owing to wild collection pressure — source captive-bred specimens where available.
breedingNotes: Breeding the Zebra Otocinclus in captivity is rarely accomplished, though spawning has been documented in home aquariums. The male initiates courtship with a characteristic T-position approach similar to that seen in corydoras catfish. Adhesive eggs — deposited singly or in small clusters of 30 to 50 — are placed on broad plant leaves and hardscape surfaces. No parental care follows spawning, and parents may consume the eggs if left in the tank. Eggs hatch in approximately 3 days at 24°C. Fry are extremely small and tend to require green water, infusoria, or biofilm-coated surfaces as their first food; standard powdered fry foods are often ignored. Fry mortality is typically high without a well-established algae culture in the rearing tank.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Otocinclus cocama
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (40–45 mm SL), temperature (21–25°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (36–179 ppm ≈ gH 2–10), tank base dimensions (45×30 cm minimum), diet (aufwuchs, algae, biofilm), native range (lower Ucayali and Marañón, Peru), habitat (dense marginal vegetation, clear water), breeding behaviour, and compatibility guidance. Group minimum of 4–6 recommended.

additional.name: FishBase — Otocinclus cocama
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 4.4 cm SL), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Peru — lower Ucayali and Marañón), habitat (clear-water creeks with marginal vegetation), IUCN Endangered status, facultative air-breathing note, and high tooth count distinguishing this species. Temperature and pH data consistent with SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Otocinclus Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade and husbandry source. Confirmed temperature 21–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, mature-tank requirement, aufwuchs-grazer diet (algae wafers, Repashy Soilent Green, blanched zucchini), group-keeping, and compatibility with dwarf shrimp and small community fish. Notes O. cocama as one of three commonly traded Otocinclus species.

additional.name: Aquariadise — Zebra Otocinclus Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Confirmed hardness up to 12 dGH, group size of 6–8, lifespan 3–5 years (conservative estimate used), ~$15 per fish retail price, IUCN Endangered status, predominantly wild-caught with high post-purchase mortality risk, and drip-acclimation recommendation.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding is rarely accomplished in home aquariums. The male initiates courtship with a T-position approach similar to corydoras catfish, and adhesive eggs — typically 30 to 50 per clutch — are deposited on broad plant leaves. Eggs generally hatch in around 3 days at 24°C. Fry are very small and tend to reject standard powdered foods, relying instead on green water, infusoria, or biofilm-coated surfaces; fry mortality is high without a well-established algae culture in the rearing tank.
